parentQuestion: Select the statement that is most correct.

options: Cells that carry electrical signals in the nervous system
scaffolds.explain: NEURONS are specialized cells that transmit ELECTRICAL IMPULSES. They have a cell body, dendrites (receiving signals), and an axon (sending signals).

wrongExplains.0: Fat-storing cells are adipocytes. Neurons transmit electrical signals.
wrongExplains.2: Hormone-producing cells are in endocrine glands. Neurons carry electrical signals.
wrongExplains.3: Infection-fighting cells are immune cells (like lymphocytes). Neurons are for signaling.

scaffolds.q: What does the cell body (soma) of a neuron contain?
scaffolds.explain: The CELL BODY (SOMA) is the neuron's control center. It contains the NUCLEUS and keeps the cell alive. The axon is just the long extension that carries signals away.

wrongExplains.0: The axon terminal is at the end of the axon, separate from the cell body.
wrongExplains.2: Myelin wraps around the axon. The cell body contains the nucleus and organelles.
wrongExplains.3: The cell body is definitely not hollow — it's packed with the nucleus and organelles.

scaffolds.q: What does the CNS (Central Nervous System) include?
options: Only the nerves in your arms and legs
All the ganglia in the body
scaffolds.explain: The CNS is the 'central command' — the BRAIN and SPINAL CORD. Everything else (nerves throughout your body) is the PNS (Peripheral Nervous System).

wrongExplains.0: Arm and leg nerves are part of the PERIPHERAL nervous system. CNS = brain + spinal cord.
wrongExplains.2: Autonomic nerves are part of the PNS. The CNS is brain and spinal cord.
wrongExplains.3: Ganglia are in the PNS (outside the CNS). The CNS is brain and spinal cord.

options: A cluster of neuron cell bodies inside the brain
A cluster of neuron cell bodies OUTSIDE the brain and spinal cord
A blood vessel in the brain
scaffolds.explain: GANGLIA are collections of neuron cell bodies in the PERIPHERAL nervous system — outside the CNS. They look like little bumps or swellings along nerves.

wrongExplains.0: Cell body clusters INSIDE the CNS are called 'nuclei,' not ganglia.
wrongExplains.2: Hormones are chemical messengers. Ganglia are nerve cell body clusters.
wrongExplains.3: Blood vessels are part of the cardiovascular system. Ganglia are nerve structures.

scaffolds.q: In nervous system terminology, what is a 'nucleus'?
options: A cluster of neuron cell bodies INSIDE the brain or spinal cord
A cluster of cell bodies outside the CNS
scaffolds.explain: In neuroanatomy, a NUCLEUS is a cluster of cell bodies INSIDE the CNS. Same structure outside the CNS = ganglion. Location determines the term!

wrongExplains.0: That's physics/chemistry. In neuroanatomy, nucleus means a CNS cell body cluster.
wrongExplains.2: Muscle cells are myocytes. A neuroanatomical nucleus is a nerve cell body cluster.
wrongExplains.3: Outside the CNS, cell body clusters are called GANGLIA, not nuclei.

scaffolds.q: What does the dorsal root ganglion (DRG) contain?
scaffolds.explain: The DORSAL ROOT GANGLION (DRG) contains cell bodies of SENSORY NEURONS. These neurons detect touch, pain, temperature, and position. Motor neuron bodies are in the spinal cord.

wrongExplains.0: Motor neuron cell bodies are in the ventral horn of the spinal cord, not in ganglia.
wrongExplains.2: While blood vessels supply the DRG, its main contents are sensory neuron cell bodies.
wrongExplains.3: The DRG is neural tissue containing sensory neuron cell bodies, not muscle.

options: Afferent = toward CNS (sensory); Efferent = away from CNS (motor)
Afferent = away from CNS; Efferent = toward CNS
scaffolds.explain: AFFERENT = ARRIVING (sensory, toward CNS). EFFERENT = EXITING (motor, away from CNS). These terms describe signal direction — a fundamental concept in neuroscience.

wrongExplains.0: They are opposites. Afferent is toward CNS; efferent is away from CNS.
wrongExplains.2: That's backwards. Afferent = arriving/toward. Efferent = exiting/away.
wrongExplains.3: Both terms specifically relate to signal direction relative to the CNS.

scaffolds.q: What type of neuron cell bodies do afferent ganglia contain?
scaffolds.explain: AFFERENT = sensory. GANGLIA = cell body clusters outside CNS. So afferent ganglia (like dorsal root ganglia) contain SENSORY neuron cell bodies. Motor neuron bodies are in the spinal cord.

wrongExplains.0: Motor neuron cell bodies are in the spinal cord (CNS), not in peripheral ganglia.
wrongExplains.2: Ganglia are segregated. Afferent ganglia contain sensory neurons only.
wrongExplains.3: Ganglia definitely contain neuron cell bodies — that's what they are.

scaffolds.q: Is the dorsal root ganglion a motor structure?
options: No, it is a SENSORY structure containing sensory neuron cell bodies
Yes, it contains motor neuron cell bodies
It has nothing to do with neurons
scaffolds.explain: The dorsal root ganglion is SENSORY, not motor. It contains cell bodies of sensory neurons. Motor neuron cell bodies are in the ventral horn of the spinal cord (inside CNS, so they're in nuclei, not ganglia).

wrongExplains.0: Motor control comes from the ventral horn. The DRG is purely sensory.
wrongExplains.2: Motor neuron cell bodies are in the spinal cord, not in the DRG.
wrongExplains.3: The DRG is entirely about neurons — specifically sensory neuron cell bodies.
